statement: LlamaIndex is a data framework for LLM applications that treats indexing as a first-class citizen: rather than bolting retrieval onto an LLM call, it structures the entire pipeline around ingestion, index construction, and query-time retrieval. This positions it as infrastructure, not a prompt library — the core value proposition is making arbitrary data sources queryable by LLMs with minimal glue code.

LlamaIndex's core object model has three layers: (1) Document — a generic container wrapping any data source (PDF, API response, database row); (2) Node (specifically TextNode, subclassed from BaseNode) — a chunk of a Document that carries text, metadata, and inter-node relationship pointers (prev/next/parent); (3) Index — a structure built over Nodes that enables retrieval (VectorStoreIndex stores each Node plus its embedding, SummaryIndex stores Nodes as a sequential chain, TreeIndex builds a hierarchy, KeywordTableIndex maps keywords to Nodes). NodeParsers bridge Documents to Nodes by chunking and inheriting metadata downward.

LlamaIndex exposes three distinct querying interfaces with different scopes: Retriever fetches the most relevant Nodes from an Index given a query — it is the foundational building block with no synthesis; QueryEngine wraps a Retriever plus a ResponseSynthesizer to answer a single standalone natural-language question in one shot; ChatEngine adds a conversation buffer on top of a QueryEngine, maintaining message history across turns for multi-turn dialogue. Choose Retriever when you own synthesis logic, QueryEngine for single-shot Q&A pipelines, ChatEngine for conversational agents.

VectorStoreIndex, KnowledgeGraphIndex, and PropertyGraphIndex serve fundamentally different retrieval semantics: VectorStoreIndex embeds each Node and retrieves by cosine similarity — best for open-ended semantic search over unstructured text. KnowledgeGraphIndex extracts subject-predicate-object triplets and stores them in a graph — suited for structured entity-relationship queries but is limited in expressiveness and is now considered legacy. PropertyGraphIndex (introduced after KnowledgeGraphIndex) uses a labeled property graph with schema-guided or free-form LLM extraction, supports multiple kg_extractors per chunk, and enables far richer relationship modeling — preferred when graph structure matters. Mixing index types for the same corpus via RouterQueryEngine is a valid production pattern.

LlamaIndex ships over 300 integration packages on PyPI (llama-index-llms-openai, llama-index-embeddings-huggingface, llama-index-vector-stores-chroma, etc.). pip install llama-index installs llama-index-core plus a minimal default set, but does NOT include most integrations. Developers who assume pip install llama-index gives them a working Chroma or HuggingFace embedding setup hit ImportError at runtime. The correct pattern is: install llama-index-core explicitly, then add each integration package individually. Namespace convention distinguishes them: imports containing .core. come from llama-index-core; imports without .core. come from a separate integration package.

The choice of embedding model silently determines retrieval quality in ways LlamaIndex does not surface at runtime. The default embedding model (text-embedding-ada-002, 1536 dimensions) is set globally via Settings.embed_model; if a user loads a pre-built index using one embedding model but queries it with a different one — or changes models between indexing and retrieval — retrieved nodes will be semantically incoherent without any error. Switching to a local model (e.g., HuggingFace BAAI/bge-small-en) changes output dimensionality, causing vector dimension mismatch errors in existing stores. Additionally, ada-002 has been superseded by text-embedding-3-small/large with better retrieval benchmarks at lower cost, but LlamaIndex's default has not always tracked this, leaving new users on a suboptimal default.

FAILURE: The v0.10 package rename from llama_index to llama-index-core broke a massive base of tutorials, blog posts, and Stack Overflow answers. Pre-v0.10 code uses `from llama_index import ...` or `import llama_index`; post-v0.10 the canonical import is `from llama_index.core import ...`. The legacy package llama-index-legacy (pinned to 0.9.48) still exists on PyPI, so pip install llama-index-legacy succeeds silently — meaning a project can have both llama_index (legacy) and llama_index.core (current) installed simultaneously, causing subtle version conflicts. ServiceContext (the pre-v0.10 configuration object) was fully deprecated; its replacement is the module-level Settings singleton. The migration CLI tool (`llamaindex-cli upgrade-file`) exists but is not widely known, leaving most users doing manual find-replace.

FAILURE: LlamaIndex's default chunk_size of 1024 tokens and chunk_overlap of 20 tokens are rarely optimal and produce poor retrieval quality on most real-world corpora without explicit tuning. The 20-token overlap is too small to preserve semantic continuity at chunk boundaries, causing split reasoning chains to be retrieved as incoherent fragments. For dense technical documents, 1024 tokens often exceeds the semantic unit size, embedding noise into vectors and lowering similarity scores for correct matches. The fix is domain-specific: start at 512 tokens with ~50 overlap, then evaluate using a retrieval benchmark. Critically, changing chunk_size on an existing VectorStoreIndex requires full re-indexing — the index does not warn users that stale embeddings from a prior chunk_size are still in use, making silent quality regression a common production failure.

LlamaIndex's IngestionPipeline is the production-grade document processing abstraction that adds deduplication, caching, and transformation chaining on top of raw NodeParsers. It caches transformed nodes by document hash so that re-running an ingestion over unchanged files is a no-op. The UNSAID production pattern: pair IngestionPipeline with a persistent DocStore (SimpleDocumentStore backed to disk, or a database store) and a persistent VectorStore — this enables incremental indexing where only modified documents trigger re-embedding. Without a DocStore, every IngestionPipeline.run() call re-processes all documents regardless of cache, causing redundant embedding API calls and silent cost accumulation on large corpora.

Two-stage retrieval (vector similarity → reranker) dramatically outperforms single-stage vector retrieval for most RAG applications, and LlamaIndex makes this composable via postprocessors. The pattern: retrieve top-K=20-50 candidates from the VectorStoreIndex using fast approximate nearest neighbor search, then rerank with a cross-encoder (CohereRerank, SentenceTransformerRerank, or LLMRerank) to select the final top-k=3-5. Cross-encoders evaluate query-document pairs jointly (vs bi-encoders that embed independently), producing much higher precision at the cost of O(K) inference calls. The UNSAID constraint: LLMRerank (using an LLM to score relevance) is the highest quality but most expensive option; for production cost sensitivity, use a dedicated reranker model (Cohere rerank-english-v3.0, or a local cross-encoder) instead of consuming LLM tokens for reranking.

LlamaIndex's metadata filtering API (MetadataFilter, MetadataFilters) is a portability abstraction over per-vector-store filter syntax, but implementation completeness varies significantly. Pinecone supports all filter operators; Chroma supports a subset; FAISS's in-memory store has no metadata filtering support at all (filters are ignored silently). The failure pattern: a developer builds and tests filtering with Chroma, deploys to FAISS for cost reasons, and discovers that document-type or date-range filters produce no narrowing — all nodes are returned regardless of filter. Always verify metadata filter support for your specific vector store in the LlamaIndex integration docs before building filter-dependent retrieval logic. Use explicit pre-filtering via a retriever wrapper when the vector store does not support native filters.

LlamaIndex QueryEngine uses response synthesis modes that trade quality for cost and latency. `refine` mode sends each retrieved chunk to the LLM sequentially, asking it to refine the answer — highest quality but O(k) LLM calls. `compact` mode consolidates chunks into fewer prompts first, reducing calls by ~50%. `tree_summarize` builds a summary tree and is best for long-document summarization. `simple_summarize` truncates to the context window — lowest cost but silently drops context. `no_text` returns only the source nodes without synthesis — useful when you need raw retrieval output for custom processing. The UNSAID default: QueryEngine defaults to `compact` mode, which is a reasonable production balance. Teams that switch to `refine` for quality without awareness of the O(k) cost multiplication often see 5-10x higher API costs on queries that retrieve many chunks.

LlamaIndex Workflows (introduced in v0.10.x) are an event-driven orchestration primitive that supersedes AgentRunner and custom orchestration logic for multi-step pipelines. Unlike QueryEngine (single-shot Q&A) or AgentRunner (ReAct loop), Workflows define explicit step functions connected by event types, enabling branching, parallelism, and human-in-the-loop interrupts. The WHY this exists: complex RAG pipelines (query decomposition → parallel sub-queries → result fusion → citation extraction) cannot be expressed cleanly as a linear QueryEngine chain. Workflows also integrate with LlamaIndex's instrumentation layer for step-level observability. The migration signal: if your QueryEngine subclass overrides more than one method, rewrite it as a Workflow.

LlamaCloud is a managed platform built on LlamaIndex that handles document parsing, indexing, and retrieval as a service. The critical distinction for self-hosted deployments: some LlamaIndex documentation examples reference `llama_cloud` imports and LlamaCloud-specific readers that require a LlamaCloud API key and send your documents to LlamaAI's servers. This is a data privacy concern for regulated industries. The open-source alternative paths are: use LlamaParse (document parsing) which has a free tier but also sends data to LlamaAI; use completely local parsers (pypdf, docling, unstructured) with the open-source LlamaIndex pipeline. Teams building air-gapped or HIPAA-compliant systems must audit all import paths for `llama_cloud` and `llama_parse` references before deployment.

LlamaIndex query engines and retrievers expose both sync and async APIs (query() vs aquery(), retrieve() vs aretrieve()), but many built-in retrievers and response synthesizers only implement the sync path — the async methods fall back to running sync code in a thread executor. This means calling aquery() in a FastAPI endpoint does not actually free the event loop during LLM I/O; it occupies a thread. To verify whether a component is truly async, check whether it overrides `_aretrieve` or `_aget_response`. True async performance requires using providers and retrievers that implement native async (OpenAI async client, Pinecone async client). Mixing sync-behind-async components in a high-concurrency FastAPI service causes thread pool exhaustion under load.

LlamaIndex Nodes carry relationship pointers (PREVIOUS, NEXT, PARENT, CHILD, SOURCE) that enable context-aware retrieval patterns. The most impactful production technique is 'sentence window retrieval': parse documents into small sentence-level nodes (high retrieval precision), but when a node is retrieved, expand the context window by fetching its PREVIOUS/NEXT neighbors before synthesis. This is implemented via MetadataReplacementPostProcessor which swaps the retrieved node's text with its surrounding window. The UNSAID requirement: window metadata must be embedded into nodes at index time using the SentenceWindowNodeParser — retro-fitting window context onto a pre-built index is not possible without re-indexing. This technique consistently outperforms fixed chunk-size retrieval on benchmarks because it decouples retrieval granularity from synthesis context length.

RAG evaluation requires measuring three independent dimensions: retrieval quality (are the right nodes retrieved?), faithfulness (does the answer stay grounded in the retrieved context?), and answer relevance (does the answer address the question?). LlamaIndex integrates with RAGAS and its own `llama_index.evaluation` module which provides `FaithfulnessEvaluator`, `RelevancyEvaluator`, and `RetrieverEvaluator` using an LLM-as-judge approach. The UNSAID production constraint: LLM-as-judge evaluation costs money and is itself subject to judge model bias — GPT-4o judge scores for GPT-4o-generated answers show systematic favoritism (same-model bias). The correct evaluation baseline is a labeled golden dataset: for each test question, store the ground-truth answer and the expected source node IDs, then measure context recall (fraction of expected nodes retrieved) and exact-match or BERTScore on the answer. Without a golden dataset, you are measuring relative quality between configuration variants, not absolute quality.

ChatEngine has multiple modes that differ fundamentally in how history affects retrieval. `best` mode (CondensePlusContextChatEngine) rewrites the user's current query using conversation history into a standalone question before retrieving — this is the production-recommended mode because retrieval is done on a disambiguated query rather than a bare follow-up like 'tell me more'. `context` mode simply prepends chat history to the retrieved context without query rewriting, causing retrieval to miss relevant documents when the question depends on prior turns. `simple` mode ignores history entirely for retrieval. The UNSAID failure: teams using `context` mode observe that follow-up questions about previously mentioned topics return irrelevant nodes because the bare follow-up question has no semantic overlap with the relevant documents. Switching to `best` mode eliminates this by making one extra LLM call per turn for query condensation — a worthwhile trade-off for conversational applications.

Custom retrievers in LlamaIndex are implemented by subclassing `BaseRetriever` and overriding `_retrieve(query_bundle: QueryBundle) -> list[NodeWithScore]`. QueryBundle contains both the query string and a pre-embedded query vector, enabling the custom retriever to use either for lookup. The UNSAID pattern for combining multiple retrieval strategies: implement a fusion retriever that calls multiple sub-retrievers, deduplicates results by node_id, and applies Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) scoring to combine ranked lists. LlamaIndex ships `QueryFusionRetriever` for this exact case, which also supports query rewriting (generating N paraphrased queries, retrieving for each, then fusing). The critical implementation trap for custom retrievers: `_retrieve` must be synchronous; `_aretrieve` is the async override. Many developers only implement `_retrieve` and assume `aretrieve()` works — it does via thread executor fallback, but this blocks the event loop in async contexts and must be explicitly overridden for truly non-blocking async operation.

Metadata filtering in LlamaIndex VectorStoreIndex improves retrieval precision by pre-filtering the candidate pool before cosine similarity ranking. The correct ingestion pattern is to embed structured metadata at indexing time as Node.metadata dict fields and use `MetadataFilters(filters=[MetadataFilter(key='doc_type', value='contract', operator=FilterOperator.EQ)])` at query time. The UNSAID performance implication: metadata filtering happens at the vector store level (before similarity computation) in databases that support it (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant) — reducing the search space from millions to thousands of documents before ANN search. For vector stores without native metadata filtering (FAISS, simple in-memory), LlamaIndex applies filtering post-retrieval, which means the full ANN search still runs and then results are dropped — negating the performance benefit. Additionally, injecting metadata into the embedding text using `MetadataMode.EMBED` (which prepends key-value pairs to the text before embedding) can improve recall by making metadata part of the semantic space, useful for filtering on fields the user may express in natural language (date, author, category).

Hybrid search combines dense vector retrieval (semantic similarity) with sparse BM25 retrieval (keyword matching) to cover both exact-match and semantic-match queries. LlamaIndex provides `BM25Retriever` for sparse retrieval over in-memory node sets and `QueryFusionRetriever` with `mode=RECIPROCAL_RERANK` to fuse results. The WHY hybrid outperforms pure vector: vector retrieval fails for queries containing rare proper nouns, product codes, or technical identifiers (e.g., 'CVE-2024-3094') because their embeddings are trained on distribution statistics and rare terms are underrepresented. BM25 retrieves documents by exact term frequency regardless of semantic similarity. The production deployment constraint: LlamaIndex's BM25Retriever builds the BM25 index in-memory from the node corpus at instantiation time — it is not persisted. For large corpora, rebuilding the BM25 index on every service restart is expensive. The solution is to use a vector store that natively supports hybrid search (Weaviate, Qdrant, Elasticsearch) so both retrieval modes share a single persistent index rather than requiring two separate indexes.

LlamaIndex's IngestionPipeline accepts a list of transformations that are applied sequentially: typically [NodeParser, MetadataExtractor(s), EmbeddingModel]. The UNSAID production insight is that transformation order matters: metadata extractors (TitleExtractor, QuestionsAnsweredExtractor, SummaryExtractor) make LLM calls per node and should be placed before the embedding step so that extracted metadata is embedded into the text representation. QuestionsAnsweredExtractor generates hypothetical questions a node answers and appends them to the node's metadata — this improves retrieval by expanding the semantic coverage of each node (a technique called HyDE-adjacent). The cost trap: running QuestionsAnsweredExtractor on 10,000 nodes with GPT-4o at 5 questions/node costs ~$15-50 in API calls for a one-time indexing operation. Use cheaper models (gpt-4o-mini or a local LLM) for metadata extraction, reserving expensive models for answer synthesis at query time.

LlamaIndex's ResponseSynthesizer uses prompt templates for each synthesis step that can be customized via `get_response_synthesizer(text_qa_template=..., refine_template=...)`. The default QA prompt asks the LLM to 'answer the question given the context' without citation or confidence instructions — for production RAG, always override the text_qa_template to (1) require inline citations using node metadata (e.g., 'cite the source document name for each claim'), (2) instruct the model to respond with 'I don't know' when context is insufficient rather than hallucinating, and (3) specify the output format (bullet points, numbered list, prose). The UNSAID failure mode of default prompts: the default refine prompt says 'refine the original answer using the new context' without instructing the model to preserve prior citations, causing the refined answer to drop citations from earlier context windows. Custom refine prompts must explicitly carry citation instructions through every refinement step.
